one_line_takeaway: Prompt-only attackers can hijack how a planner-executor multi-agent LLM system decomposes and routes a task -- not just what agents say -- by combining subtask-targeted sycophantic arguments with dependency-steering language, defeating post-hoc graph/topology defenses and requiring instead an input-side intent-decontamination defense (FLOWGUARD) applied before planning.
key_finding: Sycophantic framing amplifies structural steering non-additively: for the naive malicious argument the sycophantic gain is modest, but for task-aware arguments aligned to the highest-influence subtask, sycophantic framing raises MASR from 27.78% to 57.41% (fixed-topology) / 21.30% to 57.41% (auto-topology) on MisinfoTask with GPT-4o-mini -- consistent with the abstract's headline 'up to 37%' framing gain and the paper's reported 'up to 55%' improvement of FLOWSTEER over naive prompting. Across Table 2 (4 model families: GPT-4o, Gemini-2.5, Qwen-3.5, DeepSeek; 3 capability configs G1/G2/G3; 2 benchmarks MisinfoTask and ASB-Bench), FLOWSTEER consistently and substantially outperforms Naive Malicious prompting (NM) and generally outperforms Direct Goal Injection (DGI), e.g. GPT-4o/G1/MisinfoTask: MASR rises from 29.63% (NM) to 65.74% (FLOWSTEER); Gemini-2.5/G2/MisinfoTask: MASR from 13.89% (NM) to 75.00% (FLOWSTEER, +61.11pp). Existing post-hoc MAS defenses fail against it: ARGUS and G-Safeguard produce inconsistent, sometimes negative protection (e.g. G-Safeguard on Gemini-2.5-Flash/MisinfoTask INCREASES TASR from 63.89% to 75.92%, MASR from 63.89% to 73.15%). FLOWGUARD, by contrast, is the only tested defense with consistent, large residual-ASR reductions across all 4 models and both benchmarks -- up to 34.26pp MASR reduction (DeepSeek-V3, MisinfoTask: 56.48% to 22.22%) -- while an ablation (Figure 3) shows structural priors alone are insufficient and can even help performance without semantic contamination, and sycophantic rewriting of a naive argument gives limited gains alone; the combination of task-aware + sycophantic + dependency-guided steering is what drives the effect.
core_contribution: The paper identifies a new attack surface in planner-executor multi-agent LLM systems (MAS): workflow FORMATION itself, rather than post-formation internals (agents, tools, memory, messages). It (1) uses a social-influence-based diagnostic to show that a malicious signal's effect depends on which subtask it enters (structural sensitivity) and whether it is framed sycophantically (framing vulnerability); (2) proposes FLOWSTEER, a prompt-only attack that combines a task-aware sycophantic argument (placed at the highest-influence subtask v*, identified via a social-influence score SI(vi)) with dependency-guided workflow steering (natural-language guidance biasing the planner toward propagation-favorable dependencies e+ and away from e-, computed via a propagation score PS over active communication rounds); and (3) proposes FLOWGUARD, an input-side defense that decomposes the prompt into task/methodological/framing intent components and rewrites it to strip workflow-contaminating cues while preserving task objectives, applied BEFORE planning (unlike existing post-hoc graph/topology defenses).
threat_model: Prompt-only attacker submitting a single perturbed task t~ = t (+) a through the ordinary user-facing prompt interface of an encapsulated planner-executor MAS service. The attacker cannot hijack executor agents, modify memory/tools, alter inter-agent messages, force a topology, or inspect the generated workflow during execution/online attack. Attack construction relies on an OFFLINE profiling phase with diagnostic white-box access (clean-task workflows, traces, intermediate outputs) to derive vulnerability priors (influential subtasks, propagation-favorable dependencies), which the paper argues is practically grounded because agent frameworks commonly expose workflow structure/traces for debugging/observability. Attack execution itself, however, is purely prompt-only/black-box: the attacker submits one crafted prompt and observes only the final MAS output. The paper additionally shows (Appendix F.1, not verified against full text here) that a fully black-box variant can approximate the needed structural priors from planner outputs alone without white-box profiling access.
limitations: Vulnerability priors are profiled offline using white-box access to a single clean MAS setup (GPT-4o-mini) and one benchmark topology before being transferred/tested across other model families and configurations; the paper argues this transfer works well but the online attacker still ultimately depends on priors extracted under an access assumption (workflow trace visibility) that not all deployed MAS APIs will expose, though Appendix F.1 (not read in this pass) reportedly shows a black-box structure-inference fallback. DGI (a simpler baseline) sometimes matches or exceeds FLOWSTEER on ASB-Bench under G2/G3, attributed to ASB-Bench's open-ended tasks treating explicit goals as acceptable user preferences -- i.e., FLOWSTEER's advantage is benchmark- and task-type-dependent rather than uniform. All experiments use GPT-4o-mini as the default executor/planner backbone for probing and MisinfoTask (108 tasks) as the primary probing benchmark, with ASB-Bench being an author-constructed augmentation of 50 ASB tasks into 100 samples rather than an independently validated benchmark. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.11514v1, cs.CR).

datasets_used: MisinfoTask (from ARGUS paper [33]): 108 constrained analytical tasks with task-specific malicious arguments and targets

ASB-Bench: author-constructed from ASB [61], 50 open-ended professional tasks across 10 domains augmented with malicious arguments/targets following the MisinfoTask formulation, yielding 100 evaluation samples
quantitative_results: Sycophantic framing on task-aware high-influence argument (aSF_H) raises fixed-topology MASR from 27.78% (aH) to 57.41% (aSF_H) and auto-topology MASR from 21.30% to 57.41% on MisinfoTask/GPT-4o-mini (Table 1)
GPT-4o/G1/MisinfoTask: MASR NM=29.63%, DGI=35.18%, FLOWSTEER=65.74% (+36.11pp over NM) (Table 2)
Gemini-2.5/G2/MisinfoTask: MASR NM=13.89%, FLOWSTEER=75.00% (+61.11pp over NM) (Table 2)
GPT-4o/G1/ASB-Bench: MASR NM=13.00%, DGI=36.00%, FLOWSTEER=59.00% (+46pp over NM) (Table 2)
FLOWGUARD residual MASR after defense on GPT-4o-mini/MisinfoTask: 65.74% (undefended) to 35.19% (-30.55pp); vs ARGUS 61.11% (-4.63pp) and G-Safeguard 63.89% (-1.85pp) (Table 3)
FLOWGUARD residual MASR on DeepSeek-V3/MisinfoTask: 56.48% to 22.22% (-34.26pp), the largest reduction reported (Table 3)
G-Safeguard INCREASES TASR/MASR on Gemini-2.5-Flash: TASR 63.89%->75.92% (+12.03pp), MASR 63.89%->73.15% (+9.26pp) (Table 3) -- illustrates existing defense failure
Structural graph similarity (Gsim) between fixed- and auto-topology workflows under attack ranges 0.58-0.64 across variants (Table 1), showing replanning substantially alters the workflow
Overall headline: FLOWSTEER increases MASR by up to 55% over naive prompting; FLOWGUARD reduces MASR by up to 34% (abstract, consistent with Table 2/3 extremes)
baselines_compared: Naive Malicious Prompting (NM) -- appends a naive misleading argument
Direct Goal Injection (DGI) -- directly appends the malicious target to the prompt
ARGUS [33] -- graph-based goal-aware correction of malicious propagation (defense baseline)
G-Safeguard [52] -- topology-guided detection/repair of anomalous MAS interactions (defense baseline)
